items.myth: Reinforcement learning means the model gets stronger feedback than in supervised learning.
items.reality: RL feedback is often weaker: sparse scalar rewards instead of detailed correct answers, plus delayed consequences that make learning harder.
items.example: A robot may receive +1 only after completing a delivery, while supervised vision gets a class label for every image.

items.myth: The agent always knows the full environment state.
items.reality: Many problems are partially observable (POMDPs): sensors give incomplete observations, and the agent must infer hidden state or use memory.
items.example: A poker agent sees its cards and public cards—not opponents' hands—so the observation is not the full game state.

items.myth: Higher immediate reward always means a better policy.
items.reality: A policy can maximize short-term reward while sacrificing long-term return; reward shaping and discounting must align with the true objective.
items.example: An agent rewarded per step for staying alive in a maze might loop forever near the start instead of reaching the goal with a larger terminal bonus.

items.myth: RLHF is unrelated to classical reinforcement learning.
items.reality: RLHF applies the same paradigm—policy (LLM), actions (tokens or responses), reward (preference model)—often with algorithms like PPO on top of supervised pre-training.
items.example: After SFT, PPO updates increase the probability of responses that score highly on a learned reward model.
